---
title: "Northern Sierra placer model — data exploration"
subtitle: "What goes into the v1 placer-Au prospectivity index, layer by layer"
author: "Sky King"
date: 2026-06-01
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-fold: true
    number-sections: true
execute:
  warning: false
---

## About this notebook

This notebook walks through the v1 northern-Sierra placer-Au prospectivity
index from raw inputs to per-cell score, with a map at every step. The
companion polished writeup lives in
[northern_sierra_placer_prospectivity.qmd](northern_sierra_placer_prospectivity.qmd);
the per-fold metric tables and audit traces live in
[internal.qmd](internal.qmd).

The v2 supervised pass (PU bagging + Random Forest + LightGBM + stacking
+ isotonic calibration, per population) trains on top of the same feature
stack shown here. It's its own notebook.

## About the region

The northern Sierra Nevada deep-gravel belt is the canonical California
placer-Au province. The Tertiary auriferous gravels were laid down by the
ancestral Yuba and American rivers before late-Tertiary uplift, then
buried under andesitic lava flows, then exhumed in places by Quaternary
incision. The hydraulic-mining era (1850s-1884) stripped the Tertiary
gravels off the interstream ridges from Malakoff Diggins south through
Dutch Flat, North San Juan, You Bet, Forest Hill, Iowa Hill, and Michigan
Bluff. Modern stream placers continue along the active Yuba, Bear, and
American rivers and their tributaries.

Two populations of placer with different geomorphic signatures:

- **Tertiary deep-gravel** — pay strata on hillside benches above the
  modern drainage, exposed by hydraulic mining pits. The hydraulic-pit
  polygons themselves serve as positive labels.
- **Quaternary modern-channel** — active stream alluvium. Positive labels
  come from MRDS placer occurrences with `dep_type` matching
  placer / alluvial / stream / gravel.

This notebook builds the same feature stack for both populations. The
Phase 1 weighted index is a single composite; Phase 2 will train one
classifier per population and fuse the calibrated rasters per-cell.

## The datasets

| Dataset | Source | Role in the model |
|---|---|---|
| 3DEP 10 m DEM | USGS 3DEP via OpenTopography | elevation, slope, TPI, the paleochannel raster's input |
| NHDPlus HR | USGS National Hydrography Dataset | stream centerlines (NHD-REM channel reference), flow routing, arbolate sum / hydroseq |
| MRDS occurrences | USGS Mineral Resources Data System | lode-Au seeds (filtered); Quaternary placer positive labels |
| Orlando 2016 Hydraulic Mine Pits | USGS DOI 10.5066/F7J38QMD | 167 pit polygons — Tertiary positive labels + proximity feature |
| CGS 2010 geology | California Geological Survey FeatureServer | bedrock lithology + Quaternary alluvium boolean |
| NGDB sediments | USGS National Geochemical Database | stream-sediment chemistry (Au + As + Sb pathfinders) |
| NURE I-ICP-MS | USGS DOI 10.5066/F7765DHF | reanalyzed NURE archive (Au + Sb + Hg) |

Every dataset is open, public-domain, and reproducible from `uv sync`
+ `python scripts/northern_sierra_placer_fetch_all.py`. Raw fetches land
in `data/raw/<dataset>/`; the assembled 250 m feature frame is at
`data/derived/features_northern_sierra_placer_250m.parquet`.

```{python}
#| label: setup
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

from pathlib import Path

import geopandas as gpd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import rasterio
from matplotlib.colors import LightSource, LinearSegmentedColormap
from shapely.geometry import Point

from ai_minerals.regions.northern_sierra_placer import NORTHERN_SIERRA_PLACER
from ai_minerals.regions._northern_sierra_anchors import ANCHOR_DISTRICTS

REGION = NORTHERN_SIERRA_PLACER
WORKING_CRS = REGION.working_crs  # EPSG:3310, California Albers (meters)

# Project root resolves regardless of where Quarto runs from.
REPO_ROOT = Path.cwd()
while not (REPO_ROOT / "pyproject.toml").exists() and REPO_ROOT.parent != REPO_ROOT:
    REPO_ROOT = REPO_ROOT.parent

FEATURES = pd.read_parquet(
    REPO_ROOT / "data/derived/features_northern_sierra_placer_250m.parquet"
)
print(f"feature frame: {FEATURES.shape[0]:,} cells × {FEATURES.shape[1]} columns")
print(f"AOI: {REGION.aoi.min_lon} to {REGION.aoi.max_lon} W, "
      f"{REGION.aoi.min_lat} to {REGION.aoi.max_lat} N  (WGS84)")
print(f"Working CRS: {WORKING_CRS}  (California Albers, meters)")
print(f"Anchor districts: {len(ANCHOR_DISTRICTS)}")

# Anchor centroids in working CRS (for overlays).
ANCHORS_GDF = gpd.GeoDataFrame(
    {"name": list(ANCHOR_DISTRICTS.keys())},
    geometry=[Point(lon, lat) for (lon, lat) in ANCHOR_DISTRICTS.values()],
    crs="EPSG:4326",
).to_crs(WORKING_CRS)

# AOI bounding polygon in working CRS — used as a clip mask + map frame.
AOI_BOX_3310 = gpd.GeoSeries(
    [REGION.aoi.polygon], crs="EPSG:4326"
).to_crs(WORKING_CRS)
XMIN, YMIN, XMAX, YMAX = AOI_BOX_3310.total_bounds

# Helper: reshape a feature column into the (row, col) raster grid.
NROWS = int(FEATURES["row"].max() + 1)
NCOLS = int(FEATURES["col"].max() + 1)

def to_grid(col: str, fill: float = np.nan) -> np.ndarray:
    arr = np.full((NROWS, NCOLS), fill, dtype=np.float32)
    arr[FEATURES["row"].to_numpy(), FEATURES["col"].to_numpy()] = (
        FEATURES[col].to_numpy(dtype=np.float32)
    )
    return arr

# Raster bounds for imshow extent (working CRS, meters).
RX_MIN = FEATURES["x"].min() - 125  # half a 250 m cell
RX_MAX = FEATURES["x"].max() + 125
RY_MIN = FEATURES["y"].min() - 125
RY_MAX = FEATURES["y"].max() + 125
EXTENT = (RX_MIN, RX_MAX, RY_MIN, RY_MAX)

# Zoomed view centered on the 7 anchor districts. The full AOI is ~220 km
# E-W by ~280 km N-S; zooming to a tight bbox around the anchor cluster
# (with a 25 km buffer for context) keeps geomorphic detail visible that
# would disappear at full-AOI extent. imshow `extent` still uses the
# full raster bounds; the xlim/ylim in style_axes crops the display.
_ax_xmin, _ax_ymin, _ax_xmax, _ax_ymax = ANCHORS_GDF.total_bounds
ZOOM_PAD_M = 25_000
ZOOM_X = (_ax_xmin - ZOOM_PAD_M, _ax_xmax + ZOOM_PAD_M)
ZOOM_Y = (_ax_ymin - ZOOM_PAD_M, _ax_ymax + ZOOM_PAD_M)

def style_axes(ax, *, title=None, full_aoi: bool = False):
    """Default crops to the anchor-district zoom; pass full_aoi=True to
    show the entire AOI (used by the region-locator map for context)."""
    if full_aoi:
        ax.set_xlim(RX_MIN, RX_MAX)
        ax.set_ylim(RY_MIN, RY_MAX)
    else:
        ax.set_xlim(ZOOM_X)
        ax.set_ylim(ZOOM_Y)
    ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.set_xticks([])
    ax.set_yticks([])
    if title:
        ax.set_title(title, fontsize=11)

# Offset table so labels don't pile up on top of each other in the dense
# northern cluster (Malakoff / North San Juan / Dutch Flat / You Bet are
# all within ~10 km). Manual offsets are simpler than adjustText for a
# fixed 7-point set.
_ANCHOR_OFFSETS = {
    "Malakoff Diggins / North Bloomfield": (6, 8),
    "North San Juan":                      (-90, 4),
    "Dutch Flat":                          (6, -10),
    "You Bet":                             (-50, 10),
    "Iowa Hill":                           (-65, 0),
    "Forest Hill":                         (6, -12),
    "Michigan Bluff":                      (6, 6),
}

def label_anchors(ax, *, fontsize=8):
    import matplotlib.patheffects as pe
    for name, geom in zip(ANCHORS_GDF["name"], ANCHORS_GDF.geometry):
        ax.plot(geom.x, geom.y, "o", color="crimson", markersize=5,
                markeredgecolor="white", markeredgewidth=0.8, zorder=10)
        short = name.split(" / ")[0]
        dx, dy = _ANCHOR_OFFSETS.get(name, (6, 6))
        ax.annotate(
            short, (geom.x, geom.y), xytext=(dx, dy),
            textcoords="offset points", fontsize=fontsize,
            color="black", fontweight="semibold",
            path_effects=[pe.withStroke(linewidth=2.4, foreground="white")],
            zorder=11,
        )
```

## The seven anchor districts

These are the seven historic Tertiary deep-gravel hydraulic-mining
districts that the Phase 1 validation gate keys off. None of them appears
in the training labels: the hydraulic-pit polygons (which are the
Tertiary positive set) are matched by NAME against MRDS records to drop
them from training. Every anchor must land in the top decile of the
Phase 1 score for the gate to pass; the v1 model gets 7/7.

```{python}
#| label: region-locator
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 8))

# Light DEM hillshade as the base.
dem_path = REPO_ROOT / "data/raw/3dep_lidar/3dep_1m_northern_sierra.ds30m.tif"
with rasterio.open(dem_path) as src:
    dem = src.read(1, out_shape=(1024, src.width * 1024 // src.height))
    dem_bounds_wgs = src.bounds
# Reproject bounds to working CRS for the imshow extent.
dem_bbox = gpd.GeoSeries(
    [gpd.GeoDataFrame(
        geometry=[Point(dem_bounds_wgs.left, dem_bounds_wgs.bottom),
                  Point(dem_bounds_wgs.right, dem_bounds_wgs.top)],
        crs=src.crs,
    ).to_crs(WORKING_CRS).unary_union.envelope]
).geometry[0]
dx0, dy0, dx1, dy1 = dem_bbox.bounds

ls = LightSource(azdeg=315, altdeg=45)
hill = ls.hillshade(np.where(np.isfinite(dem), dem, np.nanmean(dem)),
                    vert_exag=2)
ax.imshow(hill, cmap="gray", extent=(dx0, dx1, dy0, dy1), origin="upper",
          alpha=0.7, zorder=1)

# AOI box.
AOI_BOX_3310.boundary.plot(ax=ax, color="black", linewidth=1.5, zorder=5)

# Anchors.
label_anchors(ax, fontsize=8)

style_axes(ax, title="Northern Sierra placer model — AOI + 7 anchor districts",
           full_aoi=True)
ax.set_xlabel("California Albers easting (m)", fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylabel("northing (m)", fontsize=9)
# Box showing the zoom extent used by every subsequent map.
from matplotlib.patches import Rectangle
ax.add_patch(Rectangle(
    (ZOOM_X[0], ZOOM_Y[0]), ZOOM_X[1] - ZOOM_X[0], ZOOM_Y[1] - ZOOM_Y[0],
    fill=False, edgecolor="crimson", linewidth=1.2, linestyle="--", zorder=6,
))
ax.annotate("zoom extent\nfor maps below", (ZOOM_X[1], ZOOM_Y[1]),
            xytext=(-6, -14), textcoords="offset points", fontsize=8,
            color="crimson", ha="right")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Malakoff Diggins, Dutch Flat, North San Juan, You Bet, Forest Hill, Iowa
Hill, and Michigan Bluff. The southern anchors (Iowa Hill, Forest Hill,
Michigan Bluff) are roughly 80 km south of the northern cluster
(Malakoff, Dutch Flat, North San Juan), straddling the modern Bear and
American river drainages. The dashed red rectangle on the locator map
marks the zoom extent every subsequent map crops to: a ~80 km square
around the anchor cluster, where the geomorphic detail lives.

## 1. Topography: elevation, slope, TPI

The 3DEP 10 m DEM (downsampled to 30 m for processing efficiency) feeds
every morphometric feature: slope, TPI, hillshade, the paleochannel
composite's LRM and GMI components, the flow accumulation that drives
SPI and TWI, the geomorphon terrace mask.

```{python}
#| label: dem-slope-tpi
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(15, 5.5), sharey=True)

elev = to_grid("elevation")
slope = to_grid("slope")
tpi = to_grid("tpi")

im0 = axes[0].imshow(elev, extent=EXTENT, origin="upper", cmap="terrain",
                     vmin=0, vmax=3500)
plt.colorbar(im0, ax=axes[0], shrink=0.7, label="elevation (m)")
style_axes(axes[0], title="3DEP DEM (10 m, downsampled to 250 m grid)")
label_anchors(axes[0], fontsize=6)

im1 = axes[1].imshow(slope, extent=EXTENT, origin="upper", cmap="magma",
                     vmin=0, vmax=40)
plt.colorbar(im1, ax=axes[1], shrink=0.7, label="slope (deg)")
style_axes(axes[1], title="Slope")
label_anchors(axes[1], fontsize=6)

im2 = axes[2].imshow(tpi, extent=EXTENT, origin="upper", cmap="RdBu_r",
                     vmin=-200, vmax=200)
plt.colorbar(im2, ax=axes[2], shrink=0.7, label="TPI (m above local mean)")
style_axes(axes[2], title="Topographic Position Index (8-cell)")
label_anchors(axes[2], fontsize=6)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

TPI (Topographic Position Index) is each cell's elevation relative to the
mean of its 8-cell neighborhood. Positive TPI marks ridges and benches;
negative TPI marks valley floors. **All seven anchor districts sit on
benches with positive TPI** — that's the geometric signature of Tertiary
deep gravels exposed by hydraulic mining, and it's why TPI feeds the
Tertiary-terrace-likelihood composite in section 7.

## 2. Hydrology: NHD flowlines + flow-derived features

NHDPlus HR ships ~533,000 mapped flowlines covering the AOI. We use them
two ways:

- **Channel reference for the paleochannel REM** — section 6 rasterizes
  the stream-order-≥2 subset onto the DEM grid and detrends elevation
  against the nearest channel.
- **Flow-routed distance from lode-Au seeds** — section 5's
  `distance_downstream_from_lode_m` walks the network from each MRDS
  lode seed downstream via `hydroseq` ordering, sticking the seed's
  arbolate sum onto every reach below it. The omnidirectional
  Euclidean `distance_to_lode_m` is a companion that fires everywhere
  the routed variant returns NaN.

```{python}
#| label: hydrology-overlay
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))

flow_acc = to_grid("flow_acc")
ax.imshow(np.log1p(flow_acc), extent=EXTENT, origin="upper", cmap="Blues",
          vmin=0, vmax=8, alpha=0.85)

# NHD flowlines, stream-order >= 3 only (otherwise the map drowns).
nhd_path = REPO_ROOT / "data/raw/nhd_hr/nhd_flowlines_northern_sierra.gpkg"
nhd = gpd.read_file(nhd_path)
nhd_major = nhd[nhd["stream_order"].fillna(0) >= 3].to_crs(WORKING_CRS)
nhd_major.plot(ax=ax, color="navy", linewidth=0.5, alpha=0.7, zorder=4)

label_anchors(ax, fontsize=7)
style_axes(ax, title="Flow accumulation (log scale) + NHDPlus HR mapped streams (order ≥ 3)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"NHD flowlines in AOI: {len(nhd):,} total, "
      f"{(nhd['stream_order'] >= 3).sum():,} with stream_order ≥ 3")
```

The biggest blue lines on the map are the main-stem Yuba and American
rivers cutting east-west through the AOI. The anchors hover on the
benches above those modern channels, not in them — that's the
Tertiary-incision geometry. Quaternary modern-channel placers are
distributed along the blue lines themselves and along the major
tributaries.

## 3. Hydraulic mining pits — the Tertiary positive labels

Orlando et al. 2016 ([DOI 10.5066/F7J38QMD](https://doi.org/10.5066/F7J38QMD))
mapped 167 hydraulic-mining pit polygons across the northern Sierra. These
serve as the **Tertiary-population positive labels** (their centroids land
in 158 unique cells on the 250 m grid). The proximity-to-nearest-pit
raster is a Phase 1 feature in its own right.

```{python}
#| label: hydraulic-pits
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 6), sharey=True)

pits = gpd.read_file(REGION.raw_paths["hydraulic_pits"]).to_crs(WORKING_CRS)
# Clip to AOI so a statewide pit GeoPackage doesn't drag in irrelevant polygons.
pits = pits[pits.geometry.within(AOI_BOX_3310.unary_union)]

# Left: the pit polygons themselves.
elev = to_grid("elevation")
axes[0].imshow(elev, extent=EXTENT, origin="upper", cmap="terrain",
               vmin=0, vmax=3500, alpha=0.6)
pits.plot(ax=axes[0], color="orange", edgecolor="darkred", linewidth=0.5,
          alpha=0.85, zorder=5)
label_anchors(axes[0], fontsize=6)
style_axes(axes[0], title=f"Orlando 2016 Hydraulic Mine Pits  (N = {len(pits)})")

# Right: distance-to-nearest-pit (inverse of the Phase 1 feature).
pit_prox = to_grid("hydraulic_pit_proximity_m")
im = axes[1].imshow(np.minimum(pit_prox, 10_000), extent=EXTENT, origin="upper",
                    cmap="viridis_r", vmin=0, vmax=10_000)
plt.colorbar(im, ax=axes[1], shrink=0.7, label="m to nearest pit (capped at 10 km)")
label_anchors(axes[1], fontsize=6)
style_axes(axes[1], title="hydraulic_pit_proximity_m (Phase 1 feature, weight 0.18)")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"hydraulic mine pits in AOI: {len(pits):,} polygons")
print(f"cells with proximity == 0 (inside a pit): "
      f"{(FEATURES['hydraulic_pit_proximity_m'] == 0).sum():,}")
```

The pit polygon cluster along the Malakoff–Dutch Flat–Iowa Hill corridor
is the geomorphic skeleton of the model. The proximity raster (right
panel) decays away from each pit; cells within 5 km of any pit get the
strongest Phase 1 contribution from this feature.

## 4. Geology — CGS 2010 + Quaternary alluvium

California Geological Survey's 2010 statewide geologic map (the
ArcGIS-hosted FeatureServer at
`services2.arcgis.com/zr3KAIbsRSUyARHG/.../GMC_Geology`) gives a
PTYPE-coded bedrock + surficial map. We extract a single boolean from it:
**is the cell Quaternary alluvium?** That's the classical Quaternary
modern-channel placer host rock.

```{python}
#| label: geology-quaternary
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 6), sharey=True)

# Left: lithology class (coarse — the CGS PTYPE codes folded into ~30 classes).
lith = to_grid("lithology_class")
im0 = axes[0].imshow(lith, extent=EXTENT, origin="upper", cmap="tab20",
                     vmin=0, vmax=20)
plt.colorbar(im0, ax=axes[0], shrink=0.7, label="lithology class (coarse)")
label_anchors(axes[0], fontsize=6)
style_axes(axes[0], title="CGS 2010 bedrock + surficial lithology")

# Right: Quaternary alluvium boolean.
qal = to_grid("is_quaternary_alluvium")
axes[1].imshow(qal, extent=EXTENT, origin="upper", cmap="Greens",
               vmin=0, vmax=1)
label_anchors(axes[1], fontsize=6)
style_axes(axes[1], title="Quaternary alluvium mask (Phase 1 feature, weight 0.05)")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"cells flagged is_quaternary_alluvium: "
      f"{FEATURES['is_quaternary_alluvium'].sum():,} / {len(FEATURES):,} "
      f"({100*FEATURES['is_quaternary_alluvium'].mean():.1f}%)")
```

The Quaternary alluvium mask traces the active stream-valley floors. None
of the seven anchor districts is in Quaternary alluvium — they're on
hillsides above the modern drainage, in older terraced deposits. This
asymmetry is why the v1 model needs the `tertiary_terrace_likelihood`
feature in section 7: the Quaternary alluvium mask alone doesn't pick
out Tertiary-pay candidates.

## 5. MRDS lode-Au seeds + distance to lode

The Mother Lode is the canonical California gold-quartz vein system —
a north-south belt of orogenic-Au lode deposits along the Sierran
foothills. MRDS records ~6,300 lode-Au points after we filter on
`dev_stat` (drop unverified prospects) and `dep_type` (drop placer; the
leakage guard in `distance_downstream_from_lode` enforces that strictly).
Tertiary auriferous gravels and Quaternary placers both ultimately source
from this lode network.

```{python}
#| label: lode-and-distance
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 6), sharey=True)

# Left: MRDS lode seeds within AOI.
elev = to_grid("elevation")
axes[0].imshow(elev, extent=EXTENT, origin="upper", cmap="terrain",
               vmin=0, vmax=3500, alpha=0.5)
lode = gpd.read_file(REGION.raw_paths["lode_mrds"]).to_crs(WORKING_CRS)
lode_in = lode[lode.geometry.within(AOI_BOX_3310.unary_union)]
lode_in.plot(ax=axes[0], color="goldenrod", markersize=2, alpha=0.6, zorder=5)
label_anchors(axes[0], fontsize=6)
style_axes(axes[0], title=f"MRDS lode-Au seeds in AOI ({len(lode_in):,} points, "
                          f"dev_stat+dep_type filtered)")

# Right: omnidirectional distance to nearest lode-Au seed.
d2l = to_grid("distance_to_lode_m")
im = axes[1].imshow(np.minimum(d2l, 15_000), extent=EXTENT, origin="upper",
                    cmap="plasma_r", vmin=0, vmax=15_000)
plt.colorbar(im, ax=axes[1], shrink=0.7, label="m to nearest lode-Au (capped at 15 km)")
label_anchors(axes[1], fontsize=6)
style_axes(axes[1], title="distance_to_lode_m (Phase 1 feature, weight 0.20)")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

dd_finite = FEATURES["distance_downstream_from_lode_m"].notna().sum()
print(f"distance_to_lode_m: finite at {FEATURES['distance_to_lode_m'].notna().sum():,} / "
      f"{len(FEATURES):,} cells  ({100*FEATURES['distance_to_lode_m'].notna().mean():.1f}%)")
print(f"distance_downstream_from_lode_m: finite at only {dd_finite:,} / {len(FEATURES):,} cells  "
      f"({100*dd_finite/len(FEATURES):.1f}%) — see note below")
```

**Why we have two distance features.** The flow-routed
`distance_downstream_from_lode_m` walks the NHD network downstream from
each lode seed via `hydroseq` ordering. It carries the right physics for
modern-channel placers (gold flows downhill on real streams). It's NaN
at 98.3% of cells in this AOI because the Sierra Tertiary deep-gravels
are paleo-channels offset from the modern Mother Lode trend — they're
not flow-routed downstream of the lode on the current drainage network.
The omnidirectional `distance_to_lode_m` companion fires at every cell
within 25 km of any lode seed regardless of routing, and it's what
carries the lode-proximity signal at the Tertiary anchors.

## 6. Paleochannel likelihood — NHD-REM + LRM + GMI composite

The paleochannel raster is a weighted composite of three geomorphic
operators run on the DEM:

- **REM (Relative Elevation Model)** — channel-relative elevation. We
  rasterize the NHDPlus HR flowlines (stream order ≥ 2) onto the DEM
  grid, then use a Euclidean distance transform to assign each cell the
  elevation of its nearest channel cell, minus its own. High REM = the
  cell sits at or near channel-level. Weight 0.45 in the composite.
- **LRM (Local Relief Model)** — elevation minus its 100 m focal mean.
  Positive LRM marks humps and benches; negative LRM marks hollows.
  Weight 0.20.
- **GMI (Geomorphic Multiscale Index)** — NaN-aware mean of geomorphon
  terrace mask + black-top-hat (150 m structuring element) + MEP
  (multiscale elevation percentile at 250 m) + log1p(D-infinity flow
  accumulation). Captures linear depressions, persistent low points,
  and high-accumulation channels. Weight 0.35.

```{python}
#| label: paleochannel-composite
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))

pc = to_grid("paleochannel_likelihood")
im = ax.imshow(pc, extent=EXTENT, origin="upper", cmap="cividis",
               vmin=0, vmax=1)
plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, shrink=0.7, label="paleochannel likelihood [0, 1]")

# NHD major streams faintly on top.
nhd_major.plot(ax=ax, color="white", linewidth=0.3, alpha=0.5, zorder=4)

label_anchors(ax, fontsize=7)
style_axes(ax, title="paleochannel_likelihood — NHD-REM + LRM + GMI composite "
                     "(Phase 1 feature, weight 0.18)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

# At-anchor values vs raster distribution.
print()
print(f"{'anchor':38s} {'value':>7s}  percentile")
for name, geom in zip(ANCHORS_GDF["name"], ANCHORS_GDF.geometry):
    # nearest cell
    dx = FEATURES["x"] - geom.x
    dy = FEATURES["y"] - geom.y
    i = (dx * dx + dy * dy).idxmin()
    v = float(FEATURES.loc[i, "paleochannel_likelihood"])
    pct_above = 100.0 * (FEATURES["paleochannel_likelihood"] > v).mean()
    short = name.split(" / ")[0]
    print(f"  {short:36s} {v:7.3f}  ({pct_above:5.1f}% of cells score higher)")
```

The first-pass NHD-REM swap (from the prior flow-derived REM) was the
biggest single feature-engineering improvement against the
publicly-licensed riverrem default. **But notice the anchor-cell numbers
above: the paleochannel composite ALONE does not light up the seven
anchors.** Their values land at 0.17 – 0.44 — mostly below the raster
median (0.40). The composite scores modern-channel-proximity (high REM at
canyon bottoms, low LRM in linear hollows); Tertiary deep-gravels sit on
benches ABOVE the modern drainage. The composite is correctly capturing
modern paleo-channel candidates and isn't an anchor-specific feature.

That mismatch is what drove the next feature.

## 7. Tertiary terrace likelihood — the bench-above-drainage signature

The `tertiary_terrace_likelihood` feature is purpose-built for the
Sierra deep-gravel geometry. It's a geometric mean of three signals:

```
tpi_high     = normalize(tpi, clip=[p50, p99])        — bench / ridge candidate
slope_low    = max(0, 1 - slope / 15°)                — planated terrace surface
not_qal      = 1 - is_quaternary_alluvium             — not in young alluvium
ttl          = (tpi_high * slope_low * not_qal) ** (1/3)
```

The geometric mean (not the arithmetic mean) means any single component
near zero zeros out the composite. That matches the geology: a Tertiary
terrace candidate needs **all three** signatures (elevated AND low-slope
AND not-young-alluvium).

```{python}
#| label: tertiary-terrace
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))

ttl = to_grid("tertiary_terrace_likelihood")
im = ax.imshow(ttl, extent=EXTENT, origin="upper", cmap="YlOrBr",
               vmin=0, vmax=0.8)
plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, shrink=0.7, label="tertiary terrace likelihood [0, 1]")

# Pit polygons in transparent outline for ground-truth context.
pits.boundary.plot(ax=ax, color="darkred", linewidth=0.6, alpha=0.7, zorder=4)

label_anchors(ax, fontsize=7)
style_axes(ax, title="tertiary_terrace_likelihood — TPI∧slope∧not-Qal "
                     "(Phase 1 feature, weight 0.15)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"{'anchor':38s} {'value':>7s}  percentile")
for name, geom in zip(ANCHORS_GDF["name"], ANCHORS_GDF.geometry):
    dx = FEATURES["x"] - geom.x
    dy = FEATURES["y"] - geom.y
    i = (dx * dx + dy * dy).idxmin()
    v = float(FEATURES.loc[i, "tertiary_terrace_likelihood"])
    pct_above = 100.0 * (FEATURES["tertiary_terrace_likelihood"] > v).mean()
    short = name.split(" / ")[0]
    print(f"  {short:36s} {v:7.3f}  ({pct_above:5.1f}% of cells score higher)")
```

The hot bands on the map are the interstream ridges between the Yuba,
Bear, and American river canyons — exactly where the Tertiary
auriferous-gravel paleo-channels are exposed. The 167 hydraulic-pit
polygons (dark red outlines) cluster tightly on those bands. The anchor
percentiles are dramatically better than the paleochannel composite's:
most anchors now rank in the top decile on this single feature alone.

## 8. Geochemistry — NGDB + NURE + Hawkes catchment

Stream-sediment chemistry from two sources:

- **NGDB** (USGS National Geochemical Database) — heterogeneous archive
  of legacy stream-sediment samples. Coverage in the northern Sierra is
  dense in some quads, thin in others.
- **NURE I-ICP-MS** (Smith 2006, DOI 10.5066/F7765DHF) — reanalysis of
  the National Uranium Resource Evaluation archive by modern inductively-
  coupled-plasma mass spectrometry. Adds Au + Sb + Hg coverage at
  semi-quantitative limits.

We aggregate per-cell using Hawkes 1976 dual-decay catchment dilution:
each cell's score is a weighted mean of upstream samples, with the weight
falling off both by network distance (catchment travel) and by drainage
area (the "dilution correction" — a sample in a tiny tributary carries
more local information than the same value in the main stem).

```{python}
#| label: geochem-hawkes
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(13, 6), sharey=True)

# Left: NGDB+NURE sample locations colored by Au_ppm (where reported).
samples = []
for key in ("geochem", "geochem_nure"):
    p = REGION.raw_paths.get(key)
    if p and p.exists():
        g = gpd.read_file(p).to_crs(WORKING_CRS)
        samples.append(g[g.geometry.within(AOI_BOX_3310.unary_union)])
all_samples = pd.concat(samples, ignore_index=True) if samples else None

elev = to_grid("elevation")
axes[0].imshow(elev, extent=EXTENT, origin="upper", cmap="Greys",
               vmin=0, vmax=3500, alpha=0.4)
if all_samples is not None:
    all_samples.plot(ax=axes[0], color="forestgreen", markersize=1.5,
                     alpha=0.6, zorder=4)
label_anchors(axes[0], fontsize=6)
style_axes(axes[0],
           title=f"NGDB + NURE I-ICP-MS sample locations "
                 f"(N = {0 if all_samples is None else len(all_samples):,})")

# Right: Hawkes catchment-Au.
hawkes = to_grid("catchment_au_hawkes")
im = axes[1].imshow(hawkes, extent=EXTENT, origin="upper", cmap="YlGnBu",
                    vmin=0, vmax=0.3)
plt.colorbar(im, ax=axes[1], shrink=0.7, label="catchment-Au Hawkes")
label_anchors(axes[1], fontsize=6)
style_axes(axes[1], title="catchment_au_hawkes (Phase 1 feature, weight 0.13)")

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The right-panel hot zones trace the major drainages with the strongest
upstream Au accumulation. The catchment-Au Hawkes feature is the geochem
analog of the lode-distance features: it integrates the network signal
that each cell's catchment captures, rather than testing the cell itself.

## 9. Phase 1 weighted index — putting it together

The Phase 1 scorer combines the features above (plus SPI band, TWI,
ksn, geomorphon terrace mask, slope) into a single weighted-sum index:

| Feature | Weight | Direction | Cap |
|---|---|---|---|
| `distance_to_lode_m` | 0.20 | inverse | 15,000 m |
| `paleochannel_likelihood` | 0.18 | direct | — |
| `hydraulic_pit_proximity_m` | 0.18 | inverse | 5,000 m |
| `tertiary_terrace_likelihood` | 0.15 | direct | — |
| `catchment_au_hawkes` | 0.13 | direct | — |
| `is_quaternary_alluvium` | 0.05 | boolean | — |
| `distance_downstream_from_lode_m` | 0.03 | inverse | 25,000 m |
| `twi` | 0.03 | direct | — |
| `spi_band` | 0.02 | bandpass | — |
| `geomorphon_terrace_mask` | 0.02 | boolean | — |
| `slope` | 0.01 | inverse | — |

Each feature is robust-min-max'd to [0, 1] using p1/p99 clipping, then
the weighted sum is renormalized to [0, 1].

```{python}
#| label: phase1-score
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 8))

phase1_path = REPO_ROOT / "data/derived/northern_sierra_placer/phase1_index_250m.parquet"
phase1 = pd.read_parquet(phase1_path)

# Reshape into raster grid, then plot.
score_grid = np.full((NROWS, NCOLS), np.nan, dtype=np.float32)
score_grid[phase1["row"].to_numpy(), phase1["col"].to_numpy()] = (
    phase1["phase1_score"].to_numpy(dtype=np.float32)
)

im = ax.imshow(score_grid, extent=EXTENT, origin="upper", cmap="inferno",
               vmin=0, vmax=1)
plt.colorbar(im, ax=ax, shrink=0.7, label="Phase 1 score [0, 1]")

# Pit polygons + anchors for context.
pits.boundary.plot(ax=ax, color="cyan", linewidth=0.4, alpha=0.7, zorder=4)
label_anchors(ax, fontsize=7)
style_axes(ax, title="Phase 1 placer-Au prospectivity (weighted-knowledge-driven index)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

The hot zones (yellow / white) trace the Tertiary deep-gravel belt
between the Yuba and American rivers. The pit polygons (cyan outlines)
sit inside the brightest cells; the seven anchor districts (red labels)
all land in the top decile of the score distribution.

## 10. Anchor gate + held-out Lindgren-secondary validation

Two independent validation checks. The first is the **anchor gate**: the
seven historic hydraulic-mining districts (Malakoff, Dutch Flat, North
San Juan, You Bet, Iowa Hill, Forest Hill, Michigan Bluff) must each
land in the top decile of the Phase 1 score. None of them is in the
training labels (the matching hydraulic-pit polygons are excluded by
name). v1 result: **7 / 7 pass**.

The second is a **held-out secondary set**: 13 Tertiary + 5 Quaternary
historical placer districts named in Lindgren's USGS Professional Paper
73 (1911) but NOT in the anchor list, hand-geocoded into a separate
fixture and never seen by any model or scorer. The anchor-overfitting
test: do these unseen districts also land near the top? Yes —
Tertiary median decile = 0 (matches the anchors), 11 / 13 in top quintile.

```{python}
#| label: anchor-and-secondary
from ai_minerals.regions._northern_sierra_lindgren_secondaries import _TERTIARY, _QUATERNARY

# Build a one-table summary of both sets.
def decile_at(lon, lat):
    pt = gpd.GeoSeries([Point(lon, lat)], crs="EPSG:4326").to_crs(WORKING_CRS)
    x, y = pt.x.iloc[0], pt.y.iloc[0]
    dx = FEATURES["x"] - x
    dy = FEATURES["y"] - y
    i = (dx * dx + dy * dy).idxmin()
    s = float(phase1.loc[i, "phase1_score"])
    pct_above = (phase1["phase1_score"] >= s).mean()
    return min(9, int(pct_above * 10)), s

rows = []
for name, (lon, lat) in ANCHOR_DISTRICTS.items():
    d, s = decile_at(lon, lat)
    rows.append(("anchor", name, s, d))
for name, (lon, lat) in _TERTIARY.items():
    d, s = decile_at(lon, lat)
    rows.append(("lindgren_tertiary", name, s, d))
for name, (lon, lat) in _QUATERNARY.items():
    d, s = decile_at(lon, lat)
    rows.append(("lindgren_quaternary", name, s, d))

vdf = pd.DataFrame(rows, columns=["set", "name", "phase1_score", "decile"])

# Histogram of deciles per set.
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4.5))
sets = ["anchor", "lindgren_tertiary", "lindgren_quaternary"]
colors = ["crimson", "steelblue", "darkorange"]
width = 0.27
for i, (s, c) in enumerate(zip(sets, colors)):
    sub = vdf[vdf["set"] == s]
    counts = sub["decile"].value_counts().reindex(range(10), fill_value=0)
    ax.bar(np.arange(10) + (i - 1) * width, counts.values, width,
           label=f"{s} (n={len(sub)})", color=c, alpha=0.85)
ax.set_xticks(range(10))
ax.set_xticklabels([f"d{d}" for d in range(10)])
ax.set_xlabel("Phase 1 decile (d0 = top 10%, d9 = bottom 10%)")
ax.set_ylabel("count")
ax.set_title("Anchor vs. held-out Lindgren-secondary validation")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=9)
ax.grid(True, axis="y", alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print()
print("=== summary ===")
for s in sets:
    sub = vdf[vdf["set"] == s]
    top_d = (sub["decile"] == 0).sum()
    med = sub["decile"].median()
    print(f"  {s:22s} n={len(sub):2d}  median_decile={med:.0f}  "
          f"top_decile={top_d}/{len(sub)}")
```

The anchor set (red) sits entirely at d0 — the gate passes. The
Lindgren Tertiary held-out set (blue) tracks the anchors closely: median
d0, with the bulk in d0 and d1. The 2 / 13 outliers (Camptonville and
Brandy City, both at d6) sit at the northern edge of the AOI where the
`distance_to_lode_m` cap penalizes the perimeter — they're real
geological signal, not feature-engineering bugs.

The Quaternary held-out set (orange) ranks lower — median d2. Phase 1 is
currently Tertiary-tuned because all seven training anchors are Tertiary
hydraulic-mining districts. The v2 supervised pass trains one classifier
per population, and the Quaternary branch will weight the modern-channel
features (SPI band, catchment-Au Hawkes, the flow-routed
distance_downstream, the Quaternary-alluvium mask) appropriately on its
own 573 MRDS placer positives.

## What's next — v2

The v2 pass trains a per-population supervised model on this same feature
stack:

1. **PU bagging** (Mordelet-Vert positive-unlabeled bagging) as the
   one-class baseline that doesn't assume the unlabeled set is negative.
2. **Random Forest** and **LightGBM** as supervised baselines (using PU
   bagging's posterior as a pseudo-negative weighting).
3. **Stacking meta-learner** — logistic regression over (RF, LGBM)
   out-of-fold predictions.
4. **Isotonic calibration** via `CalibratedClassifierCV` (with Platt
   sigmoid fallback when a population has fewer than 30 positives).
5. **Two-population fusion** — calibrated Tertiary and Quaternary rasters
   combined per-cell with `np.maximum`, written as the deliverable
   GeoTIFF.

Every stage uses the same 20 km spatial-block cross-validation iterable —
including the stacking meta-learner's `cv` argument, so the meta-learner
itself never sees in-block test positives during fit. The leakage guards
on `distance_downstream_from_lode` (placer-flagged record refusal) and
on `catchment_au_hawkes` (per-fold recompute with held-out-sample mask)
keep the labeled-feature pipeline honest under spatial-block CV.

When v2 lands, this notebook becomes the "feature stack" appendix to the
polished
[northern_sierra_placer_prospectivity.qmd](northern_sierra_placer_prospectivity.qmd)
writeup, and a v2 notebook documents the supervised pass with the same
chart-per-step discipline.
